posts.raw_content:
In a candid discussion on The Joe Rogan Experience, Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. spoke plainly about President Trump’s February executive order aimed at increasing domestic production of glyphosate-based herbicides. 
Drawing on his decades-long track record, he stated: “I’ve spent 40 years fighting pesticides. I was part of the trial team on the Monsanto case, which was the team that won three cases in a row and then got an $11 billion settlement with Monsanto, which is now Bayer … But pesticides are poison,” he said. 
“They’re designed to kill all life. It’s not a good thing to have in your food … so it’s not something that I was particularly happy with. Let me put it that way mildly.”
He reinforced the fundamental problem: these substances are engineered to be toxic, with no appropriate role in the food supply. 
The phrase “They’re designed to kill all life” resonates powerfully for anyone who has followed his environmental and public-health advocacy over the years.
The executive order, issued under the Defense Production Act, seeks to expand manufacturing of elemental phosphorus and glyphosate-based products, citing national security and the need to maintain stability in a food system where 97% of corn and 98% of soy currently depend on glyphosate. 
RFK Jr. acknowledged the structural realities, explaining: 
“The president didn’t create this system. He’s dealing with a problem that was created long before, over the past 60 years, when, through federal policies and subsidies and the management of farming in this country—the agricultural management—we have addicted our farmers to these pesticides, and particularly glyphosate. Glyphosate is the foundational pesticide of our food production system.”
He elaborated on the scale of dependence: “So 97% of corn in this country is produced with glyphosate and can’t be produced without it… 98% of soy is produced with glyphosate. If you banned glyphosate overnight, or if you got rid of it, or if somebody else cut off our supply, it would destroy the American food system.” 
While he noted that organic methods exist (though currently representing only about 3% of corn production), he presented the order as a pragmatic measure to prevent abrupt supply-chain collapse—particularly from foreign sources—rather than an endorsement of glyphosate as safe or desirable.
Regarding the controversial immunity provision under Section 707 of the Defense Production Act, which provides limited legal protection to producers who increase output in direct compliance with the federal directive, RFK Jr. did not expressly endorse it. 
However, his broader defense of the order as a necessary response to a deeply entrenched, subsidy-driven system implicitly frames the provision as part of a short-term trade-off for supply-chain security and national resilience.
For the MAHA community that has been asking tough questions about alignment and direction—this is direct from the source. 
RFK Jr. remains the same principled advocate who has fought chemical toxins in court for decades. 
He is simply operating within a complex, 60-year-old agricultural framework that cannot be overhauled instantaneously without risking food-system stability. 
His willingness to express public discomfort, even mildly, offers some reassurance that principled disagreement and internal dialogue are still possible.
The path forward remains clear: reduce reliance on toxins, reform agricultural subsidies, and ensure accountability for chemical manufacturers. 
This moment provides some reassurance that MAHA priorities are not being fully set aside. 
As for me, I’m going to be fervently supporting Rep. Thomas Massie’s “Glyphosate Right to Know and Phase-Out Act.” 
The bill seeks to phase out the use of glyphosate in federal programs, ban its application on public lands, and eliminate federal subsidies and crop insurance incentives that currently encourage conventional farmers to rely on the herbicide. 
It’s a concrete legislative step toward breaking the cycle of addiction RFK Jr. described and giving farmers meaningful support to transition to healthier, regenerative practices. 
If you’re as concerned as I am, reach out to your representatives and let them know this matters.
posts.author: JohnyBlaze99
posts.title: RFK Jr. has finally addressed the glyphosate executive order head-on, and it turns out his concerns align closely with what so many of us have been feeling.

Kennedy Jr. spoke plainly about President Trump’s February executive order aimed at increasing domestic production of glyphosate-based herbicides. Drawing on his decades-long track record, he stated: “I’ve spent 40 years fighting pesticides.
